text.title: design-docs-big-tech Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for design-docs-big-tech. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Always include "do nothing" as Alternative 1 in the alternatives section.
rule.rationale: Sometimes the best decision is not to build. Forcing this option ensures the author justifies why building is worth the cost and risk.

rule.statement: Write in narrative prose, no bullet points, maximum 6 pages; supporting data goes in an unlimited appendix.
rule.rationale: Dense prose forces the author to synthesize; bullets permit loose thinking. Every word must carry information.

rule.statement: Use the lightest template that fits the change scope; do not over-engineer the process.
rule.rationale: Uber evolved from simple RFCs to tiered ERDs precisely because heavyweight process for lightweight changes slows teams without adding value.

rule.statement: Separate the RFC (consensus-reaching) from the ADR (decision-recording); do not conflate the two jobs in one document.
rule.rationale: An RFC trying to reach consensus and record the decision simultaneously produces a document that does neither well. The RFC is collaborative and iterative; the ADR is the immutable record.

rule.statement: Match document weight to change scope; write before coding, never after.
rule.rationale: Post-implementation documentation defeats the purpose — design docs exist to catch issues when changes are still cheap.

rule.statement: Use LLMs for initial structure, alternative brainstorming, and prose refinement; require human validation for every technical accuracy claim.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate plausible but technically inaccurate design docs when system-specific context is absent. Technical accuracy is the one dimension where LLM effectiveness is Low-Medium.

rule.statement: Limit LLM-generated alternatives to 2-3 concrete options; agents over-generate when not constrained.
rule.rationale: 5+ alternatives overwhelm reviewers and dilute focus. The value is in comparing 2-3 realistic options, not exhaustively listing all possibilities.
